coredata.dc:title: A shining transmutation of lignin into multicolor carbon dots via the dynamic solvent-fractionation engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Xinyan
Xu, Jikun
Zhou, Pengfei
Dai, Lin
Zhang, Jiaming
Xiao, Xiao
Huo, Kaifu
coredata.dc:description: As a fascinating luminescent subclass, the rapid growth of versatile carbon dots from ideally sustainable biomass has been shouldering the duty of relieving energy and environment pressures that is committed to carbon neutrality. Despite the renewable lignin with conjugated aromatic structure, tunable linkages and functional species (e.g.,–OH, –COOH and –OCH3), the fabrication of multicolor lignin-derived carbon dots (LCDs) covering the red-emissive luminescence is awkward by lacking the task-specific techniques and modulating mechanisms. Herein, a library of blue, green and red-emissive LCDs is built through a stepwise solvent-fractionation engineering with dynamic ethanol sieving and dichloromethane/water extraction. By refined lignin, it is revealed that their average molecular weights and –OH/COOH contents dominate the nanosize, reactivity, lifetime, and photophysical features of LCDs. We then showcase the gradient lignin structure to involve LCDs properties capable of driving their potential functions in the multi-target sensing and solid-state luminescent films. Monitoring tetracycline and metal-ions (Fe3+, Al3+ and/or Ag+) with the ideal selectivity and sensitivity within a broad concentration is accomplished via evaluating the linear correlations. Transparent solid-state luminescent composite films that demonstrate the promising scalability of reserving perishable fruit are successfully prepared using red-emitting LCDs as additives in polyvinyl alcohol matrix. Hence the in-depth understanding of lignin chemistry will provide a technical guidance to achieving multicolor LCDs with tailored fluorescence performances and functionalities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Solvent-fractionation
Multicolor carbon dots
Fluorescent sensing
Solid-state luminescent films

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic solvent-fractionation engineering and structural properties of lignin carbon dots
Multi-species sensing and solid-state luminescent film via lignin carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32171728 and 22008159 ), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project ( 2022020801020312 ), and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018 T110770 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram and fundamental properties of the multicolor lignin carbon dots (LCDs) using a coupled solvent-fractionation engineering. (a) A brief flowchart of preparing multicolor fluorescence LCDs (FCDs, WCDs, and DCDs) from the gradient ethanol-fractionated lignin (L50, L100, and LR). The labels L50, L100 and LR represent the lignin fractions sequentially recovered from 50 % ethanol, 100 % ethanol, and residual solid respectively. The tag FCDs corresponds to the LCDs originated from the first-step lignin acidolysis filtrate including L50-FCDs, L100-FCDs, and LR-FCDs. After acidolysis processing (sulfanilic acid), the leftover lignin was hydrothermally reacted to generate LCDs that can be further extracted via CH2Cl2/H2O; thereinto, the WCDs and DCDs refer to the water- and dichloromethane-phase extracted LCDs respectively. (b) The yield of lignin fractions after gradient ethanol precipitation. (c) The weight-average (M w) molecular weights and polydispersity index (PDI, M w/M n) of lignin fractions. (d) Quantitative functional groups (mmol/g) of hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxyl (−COOH) groups in fractionated lignin. Total P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyls. Basic structural linkages of lignin including (e) side-chain δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0 and (f) aromatic regions δC/δH 105–160/6.0–8.0 in the two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) spectrum. (g) Existent substructures of lignin identified in the 2D HSQC NMR spectra including β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4 linkages with a free –OH at the γ-carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4 substructures (A′), resinol (B, formed by β–β, α-O-γ, and γ-O-α linkages), phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β–5 and α-O-4 linkages), syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), ferulate (FA), and free p-coumaric acid (pCA) units. (h) The potential scheme of the formation mechanism from technical lignin into multicolor CDs (blue, green, and red fluorescence), mainly including acidic oxidized cleavage/N, S co-doping and aromatic refusion/deoxygenation reactions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Morphological characterizations of the multicolor emissive lignin carbon dots. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), inset HRTEM images, and particle size distributions of the L50-FCDs (a), L100-FCDs (b), and L50-DCDs (c). (d) High-angle annular dark-field scanning (HAADF) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) images of L100-FCDs (left) and L50-DCDs (right). (e) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of L50-FCDs, L50-DCDs, L100-DCDs and LR-DCDs, respectively. (f) Raman spectra of L50-FCDs and LR-DCDs (insert IG/ID values defines the ratio of D-band defect to G-band graphite). (g) Zeta potentials of the selected L100-WCDs and L50-FCDs.

Fundamental surface-state properties of the multicolor lignin carbon dots. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images of the L50-FCDs (a), L100-FCDs (b), and L50-DCDs (c). (d) Full survey X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), high-resolution XPS of (e) C 1 s, (f) O 1 s, (g) N 1 s and (h) S 2p of LR-FCDs, involving various carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur species respectively. (i) Plots of UV–vis DRS spectra to estimate band-gap energies (E g) via Kubelka-Munk formula for L50-FCDs and L100-DCDs.

Optical performance and luminescence lifespan of the multicolor lignin carbon dots. Fluorescence emission spectra of the red LCDs including (a) L50-DCDs, (b) L100-DCDs, and (c) LR-DCDs excited at different wavelengths. The PL mapping images of (d) L50-DCDs, (e) L100-DCDs, and (f) LR-DCDs. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRP) decay curves (λ ex = 400 nm) of (g) FCDs, (h) WCDs and (i) DCDs-related species. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The selective sensing of tetracycline (TC) through FCDs (lignin acidolysis filtrate-derived carbon dots). Fluorescence intensity variations for the selected (a) L50-FCDs, (b) L100-FCDs and (c) LR-FCDs upon adding different concentrations (0–500 μM) of TC, as well as, the corresponding linear fitting plot of (F0-F)/F0 versus the low TC concentration of 0–50 μM for the (d) L50-FCDs, (e) L100-FCDs and (f) LR-FCDs. Wherein F0 represents the fluorescent intensities of original LCDs and F defines the various fluorescence intensities of mixed LCDs/pollutants (e.g., TC or metal-ions) system. LOD: limit of detection.

The selective sensing of heavy metal-ions through WCDs (water-phase extracted carbon dots after lignin acidolysis followed by hydrothermal treatment). For L50-WCDs, (a) the relative fluorescence emission intensities upon adding various kinds of metal ions; the changes in fluorescence intensity upon adding different concentrations (0–800 μM) of Fe3+ (b) and the corresponding linear fitting plot (c) of (F0-F)/F0 versus Fe3+ concentration of 0–100 μM; fluorescence emission spectra upon adding different concentrations (0–500 μM and 0–10 μM) of Al3+ (d-e) and the corresponding linear fitting plot (f) of (F0-F)/F0 versus Al3+ concentration of 0–10 μM. For L100-WCDs, the relative fluorescence emission intensities (g) upon adding different kinds of metal-ions; emission spectra with varying Fe3+ from 0 to 300 μM (h) and the linear fitting relationship (i) between fluorescence quenching (F0-F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration. For LR-WCDs, the relative fluorescence emission intensities (j) upon adding different kinds of metal-ions; emission spectra with varying Ag+ from 0 to 600 μM (k) and the linear fitting relationship (i) under the Ag+ concentration of 0–200 μM.

Fundamental features and food-packaging application of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-LCDs solid-luminescent composite film using 0.5 % L50-DCDs as additive. Full XPS survey scan (a), high-resolution XPS C 1 s (b) and O 1 s (c) spectra of PVA-LCDs hybrid film. The solid-luminescent behavior (d) of PVA-LCDs composite under the UV irradiation. (e) The proposed PVA-LCDs cross-linked network through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction within as-prepared film. The water contact angle (f), SEM images (g), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (h) of PVA-LCDs composite film. (i) The comparison of PVA (top) and PVA-LCDs (down) packaging films with the ability of storing strawberry under ambient atmosphere at ∼ 25 °C. Using two kinds of storage conditions, the right magnified photos provide a visual representation of strawberry surface decayed degree after nine days.

head.ce:title: A shining transmutation of lignin into multicolor carbon dots via the dynamic solvent-fractionation engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a fascinating luminescent subclass, the rapid growth of versatile carbon dots from ideally sustainable biomass has been shouldering the duty of relieving energy and environment pressures that is committed to carbon neutrality. Despite the renewable lignin with conjugated aromatic structure, tunable linkages and functional species (e.g.,–OH, –COOH and –OCH3), the fabrication of multicolor lignin-derived carbon dots (LCDs) covering the red-emissive luminescence is awkward by lacking the task-specific techniques and modulating mechanisms. Herein, a library of blue, green and red-emissive LCDs is built through a stepwise solvent-fractionation engineering with dynamic ethanol sieving and dichloromethane/water extraction. By refined lignin, it is revealed that their average molecular weights and –OH/COOH contents dominate the nanosize, reactivity, lifetime, and photophysical features of LCDs. We then showcase the gradient lignin structure to involve LCDs properties capable of driving their potential functions in the multi-target sensing and solid-state luminescent films. Monitoring tetracycline and metal-ions (Fe3+, Al3+ and/or Ag+) with the ideal selectivity and sensitivity within a broad concentration is accomplished via evaluating the linear correlations. Transparent solid-state luminescent composite films that demonstrate the promising scalability of reserving perishable fruit are successfully prepared using red-emitting LCDs as additives in polyvinyl alcohol matrix. Hence the in-depth understanding of lignin chemistry will provide a technical guidance to achieving multicolor LCDs with tailored fluorescence performances and functionalities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It has been witnessed that the development of luminous bodies has led to the thriving innovations in multimodal energy, chemical and bioengineering fields [1,2]. As a rising star, the so-called nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) is a general term to denote dispersive quasi-0D photoluminescent (PL) materials with a tiny particle size of less than 10 nm [3–5]. They are composed of sp 2/sp 3 hybridized carbon cores with the shells of functional groups (e.g., the carboxyl and amino species), gaining tremendous attention due to their superior physicochemical capabilities including biocompatibility, water dissolubility, tunable functionality, and robust chemical durability along with electrical conductivity [6–8]. Particularly, the outstanding behaviors of quantum confinement, molecular state, and surface defects entrust the CDs with unique fluorescence property, thereby allowing their latent capacity in advanced applications including biosensing [9,10], energy storage [11,12], photocatalysis [13–15], etc.

Up to now, versatile carbon resources have been accepted to construct CDs with the ignorance of the huge potential in low-cost biomass [16,17]. Meanwhile, suffering from the exhausted fossil consumption and the associated environmental concerns, the promotion of carbon neutrality via adopting sustainable alternatives to not only alleviate global warming but also alter energy structure is urgently demanded [18,19]. Thus, it is highly inspired to explore renewable lignocellulose resources for spreading tunable multicolor luminescent CDs from blue to red light [20,21]. Betraying the perspective of trash-into-treasure, the worldwide lignin production from paper-making industry is about 50–70 million tons annually, and approximately 98 % of that is simply burned to generate heat or power [22,23]. More importantly, the high carbon proportion (>60 %), conjugated aromatic structure, alterable molecular weights, and diverse aryl ethers (e.g., β-O-4) and C–C linkages (e.g., β-β, β-5, β-1) are formed between the subunits during lignin biosynthesis to present the inherent talents in preparing multicolor CDs [23]. A rational deduction reported that the presence of lengthened molecular chain with oxygen-related functional groups, particularly ethers, is likely to expedite the reactivity and also influence the core dimensions in the initial phase of assembling CDs [24,25]. While presenting CDs with the final core–shell structure, the oxygen-rich moieties (e.g., –OCH3, –COOH, –OH) from heterogeneous lignin would be naturally decorated into the interface of CDs to create intrinsic defect and/or surface vacancies [26,27]. As a matter of fact, the dilemma of acquiring multicolor lignin-derived CDs (LCDs) with a controllable structure-performance principle is difficult to handle, which should be taken into account to inspire the prevailing light of LCDs.

In this contribution, we innovatively propose a dynamic solvent-fractionation engineering to produce multicolor LCDs with blue, green, and red fluorescence via an auxiliary system of gradient lignin dissolution followed by CH2Cl2/water extraction. Through the well-designed lignin fractionation, the tunable molecular structures of refined lignin fractions are systematically linked with their optical, lifetime, and selectively sensing properties of the as-prepared LCDs. The red-emissive LCDs can be easily nurtured within dichloromethane phase, holding the ability to construct solid-state luminescent films by adding them into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) gel. Hence the suitable coupling of multicolor LCDs with a dynamic solvent-fractionation approach that achieve lignin classification and nano-phase assembly pave a promising avenue to generate sustainable luminescent materials with versatile functions.

Industrial alkali lignin was kindly provided from Shandong Longlive Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Jinan City, China). Prior to use, crude lignin was rinsed with acidic water (pH = 2) to eliminate impurities and then oven-dried in a vacuum cabinet at 60 °C for 24 h. After purification, the chemical composition is determined to contain lignin of ∼ 96.4 wt%, total carbohydrates of ∼ 2.1 wt%, and ash of ∼ 1.5 wt%. All chemical reagents were of analytic grade, such as ethanol and sulfanilic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic solvent-fractionation engineering and structural properties of lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic heterogeneity of lignin, an abundant aromatic biopolymer in nature, ignites the inevitable challenges to extend its valorization of high-value products. Taking advantage of its heterogeneous phenolic substructures (i.e., syringyl, guaiacyl, and p-hydroxyphenyl units) and plentiful functional groups (e.g., −OH and –COOH), lignin provides the convenience of tuning fundamental carbon framework (e.g., sp 2/sp 3 hybridization) and surface modifications (e.g., defect and edge). Ameliorating the complexity of technical lignin with a uniform polydispersity is indispensable to not only discriminate the potential structure–property interplays, but also forecast and fabricate the tailor-made materials enabling versatile functionality [28,29]. In view of this, a stepwise fractionation strategy is adopted to dynamic molecular sieving of lignin via the gradient ethanol precipitation as presented in Fig. 1 a. As one of the ordinary solvents, low-cost ethanol is widely employed for the wood delignification and/or lignin fractionation in the recent works [30–32]. The inhomogeneous lignin structure with tunable functional groups and molecular weights directly dominates its solubility in a designated solvent. In the principle of so-called Alcell procedure, therefore, the configuration and properties of lignin can be sieved and/or controlled through dynamically altering the composition of ethanol/water mixture [28]. Herein we adopted the diverse ethanol/water proportions (50/50 and 100/0, v/v) to conduct a successive fractionation, thereby recycling lignin fragments with discrepant yields, molecular weights, and functional groups (Fig. 1). As reported in Fig. 1b, the major lignin fractions of L50 exhibiting a yield of ∼ 65.1 %, was dissolved in 50 % aqueous ethanol. While the leftover solids can be further sieved via using 100 % ethanol, yielding ∼ 10.9 % dissolved fragments of L100 and ∼ 20.8 % as the residual lignin (LR). Subsequently, the three types of fractionated lignins will be accepted as differentiable resources to fabricate multicolor LCDs, shedding lights on the underlying interaction between gradient lignin structure and LCDs properties.

In a nutshell, the purpose of tailoring lignin with relatively uniform fragments using dynamic solvent-fractionation approach is to grasp well-defined lignin properties that contain hydrophilic groups (e.g., −OH and –COOH), molecular weight distribution, and structured linkages, directly resulting in the fundamental configurations and optical performances of LCDs. The sequence of gradient ethanol was designed to retrieve lignin fractions with gradually increasing molecular weights and low polydispersity. From Fig. 1c, the average molecular weight (M w) of alkali lignin (AL) is determined to be approximately 2258 g mol−1 with a relatively higher polydispersity of 3.11. The SEM image (Fig. S1a, multiscale microparticles) and molecular weight profile (Fig. S1b, mainly ranging from 100 to 10,000 g mol−1) of AL testifies the heterogeneous distribution. The maximum L50 constitutes a lower M w fraction (∼1550 g mol−1) of short chains with a narrow DP (degree of polymerization), whilst the L100 presents a M w value of ∼ 2860 g mol−1. As a part of the fragments including a higher content of aliphatic alkyl chains, the residual lignin (LR, M w value of ∼ 4195 g mol−1) with a low solubility in ethanol can be detached at the end of the process. It required more polar solvents such as DMSO and pyridine, which might result in the LR difficult to further chemical transmutation. The gradient average molecular weights of refined lignin occupied a crucial role in contributing to the subsequent preparation of CDs from morphology and size to optical properties. In particular, the M w is a direct expression of the molecular size of lignin motifs that dominates the particle size distribution, surface sites, and nucleation environment of CDs, thereby endowing a homologous photoluminescence of CDs with specific emission. The calculated polydispersity of all the isolated lignin is scattered in the interval ranging from 1.8 to 2.8, which is conducive to construct the carbon-based materials from refined lignin [30,33]. In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. S1c), the functional groups of lignin (the peak at around 1240 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O in the guaiacyl units, as well as, the characteristic absorptions at ∼ 1420, 1510, and 1600 cm−1 correspond to the C=O stretching vibration of the aromatic rings) are varied as a result of gradient ethanol fractionation.

As input for the next procedure, the advanced NMR techniques (e.g., 31P and 2D HSQC) have been affirmed to be capable of deciphering structural essentials about the pivotal functional groups and complex interior subunits bonded with lignin macromolecules. The oxygen-contained reactive and/or hydrophilic groups of lignin are of great concern to decorate the LCDs, hence we performed the 31P NMR with the objective of quantifying –OH and –COOH contents as depicted in Fig. 1d. Through the gradient ethanol dissolution, the aliphatic OH content of LR is calculated to be 2.283 mmol g−1, which is obviously higher than those of AL (1.856 mmol g−1), L50 (1.279 mmol g−1), and L100 (1.694 mmol g−1). Besides the number of aliphatic alcohols is gradually increased from 1.279–2.283 mmol g−1 with magnifying M w of lignin fractions, whereas the amount of phenolic –OH (3.866–2.211 mmol g−1) is decreased. Note that aliphatic and phenolic –OH are usually located in the side-chains and aromatic C-3 position of lignin, respectively. Hence a plausible reason of the fact is contributed to the control of lignin heterogeneity, M w and short branches. The presence of carboxylic acids (2.063–1.469 mmol g−1) in fractionated lignin illustrates that an extensive oxidation has taken place at terminal groups, thus transforming the aliphatic –OH into –COOH from the technical alkali lignin process. The presence of oxygen-contained groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH and –OCH3) in lignin precursor might in-situ doping into CDs, which is propitious to hybridize carbonaceous core (e.g., the sp 2/sp 3 proportion and intrinsic defect) and modulate surface active sites (e.g., oxygen-induced edge and vacancy). The core–shell structure and photoluminescence properties of LCDs can be manipulated via tuning the reactive oxygen species from lignin. From this point of view, varying the species and contents of –OH and/or –COOH in lignin fragments holds a direct correlation to maneuver the fundamental carbon and defect structures of LCDs.

An in-depth investigation of lignin macromolecular backbone and its intrinsic nature on the linkages connecting the monomers are conducted via 2D-HSQC NMR. A variety of lignin substructures that will influence the reactivity of macromolecules can be observed through the side-chain (δC/δH 50–90/2–6, Fig. 1e) and aromatic (δC/δH 100–160/6–8, Fig. 1f) regions in the spectra, and the representative lignin motifs are illustrated in Fig. 1g. Among three methoxylated phenylpropanoid structures including syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H) units, the principal intercalated linkages are composed of ether (e.g., α-O-4 and β-O-4) and C–C (e.g., β-1, β-β, and β-5) bonds [34]. As presented in Fig. 1e, the cross-linked signals of lignin methoxyl (–OCH3) and side-chain linkages, such as β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4 linkages with a free –OH at the γ-carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4 substructures (A′), resinol (B, formed by β–β, α-O-γ, and γ-O-α linkages), and phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β-5 and α-O-4 linkages) can be well-distinguished [35]. The independent aromatic signals that correspond to three essential phenylpropanoid substructures including S, G and H units, are well-located in the Fig. 1f. The indirect evidence of oxidation germination is decided by the presence of oxidized S2,6 cross-signal. Besides, the signals originating from free p-coumaric acid (pCA) and ferulate acid (FA) units are also apparently unraveled to present an important characteristic of graminaceous lignin macromolecules in plant cell wall [36]. In the herbaceous plants, a particular structure of ether-ester bridge can be formed through bifunctional FA, wherein the polyphenol-FA complex can be not only attached to lignin with an ether bond but also bound with hemicelluloses through an ester linkage [36]. By means of dynamic ethanol fractionation, the heterogeneous lignin is easily classified into three fractions with varied M w values, branched-chains, −OH/−COOH contents, and S/G/H proportion, providing the potentially tunable environment to fabricate multicolor LCDs from blue to red light-emitting. It thus is worth forecasting that lignin seems to be well-suited for fabricating polycyclic aromatic CDs with a lignin-structure-M w related and properties.

In experimental, we carried out the acidolysis of fractionated lignin with the aid of sulfanilic acid at 90 °C for 1 h (Fig. 1a). The LCDs can be easily recovered from the filtrate as FCDs, thereby allowing the residual lignin fragments with an opportunity of extended treatment. Hence the solvothermal processing of acidolysis lignin was conducted in ethanol at 180 °C for 8 h. The possible mechanism of forming LCDs mainly concerns the acidic decomposition of lignin macromolecules and then the aromatic refusion/surface passivation of nanoparticles, which is proposed in Fig. 1h. Specifically, the acidolysis reaction of refined lignin fractions (i.e., L50, L100, and LR) with sulfanilic acid can partially cleave the aromatic ether and carbon–carbon bonds, accompanying with the possible condensation and N, S-coexisted dopants to generate new interior linkages. On this account, several types of functional groups that involves O-, N- and S-contained active species are successfully introduced into lignin fragments. Besides the direct nucleation and surface passivation of FCDs, the acidolysis intermediates from refined lignin molecules function as the multimodal precursors to construct LCDs with inequable structures and properties. Imposed by a severe condition of solvothermal processing, the reactive lignin fragments with short side-chain might be deoxygenated and restructured to generate the small fraction of LCDs under the aromatic refusion via the prompt sp 2 hybridization and π-π stacking. Meanwhile, the embedding of C−N, N−O, N−H, and C–SOx groups into the framework, interface and edge of LCDs are expedited during the solvothermal carbonization. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was filtered and then freeze-dried, followed by extraction using a mixture of dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) and water with a volume ratio of 1:1. Benefitting from nano-phase solvent extraction, the mixed LCDs can be detached into two portions that include blue-emissive LCDs in water (WCDs) and red-emissive LCDs in dichloromethane (DCDs). It is worth emphasizing that the coupled solvent-fractionations, including refined lignin properties and multicolor LCDs nano-phase extraction, not only prepare the red-emissive DCDs but also provide an opportunity to disclose the underlying relationship between well-defined lignin structures with tunable optical performance of LCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-species sensing and solid-state luminescent film via lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: It is still an urgency of acquiring substantial information to grasp the hidden rules between the photoluminescence properties and practical applications of LCDs. Herein, unraveling the fundamental structures and optical performances is irreplaceable to achieve multi-objective sensing and solid-state luminescent film. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 2 a-c) images obviously present the particle-size distributions and carbon-nuclei crystal lattices of the selected L50-FCDs, L100-FCDs, and L50-DCDs with quite different phenomena. The TEM images of L50-FCDs, L100-FCDs, and L50-DCDs illustrate the mono-dispersed conditions with the average sizes of ∼ 7.17, 7.05, and 9.92 nm with the inset high-resolution, shifting the emission wavelengths via the quantum confinement effect. With the insert high-resolution TEM images, a graphite lattice (1120) distance of 0.24 nm can be attributed to the L50-FCDs (Fig. 2a) [37], whereas the L100-FCDs (Fig. 2b) and L50-DCDs (Fig. 2c) have an identical lattice spacing of ∼ 0.26 nm corresponding to the (020) planes of graphitic carbon [38]. From the HRTEM images of L50-FCDs and L100-FCDs, the incomplete carbon cores and ambiguous crystal boundaries can be observed in their aggregates, suggesting that the formation of intermediate carbon-nuclei configuration is presented under the weakly solvothermal carbonization procedure. In contrast, the average particle size of L50-DCDs is larger than that of L50-FCDs and L100-FCDs, along with the distinct lattices capable of forming intense carbon-nuclei. This findings also affirm that polymorphic parts from the aggregates are beginning to take shape via agglomerating lignin fractions, which can be dehydrated and carbonized simultaneously to produce multiple LCDs centers. Hence the diversiform carbonized degree of LCDs with multicolor properties can be split through CH2Cl2/water extraction. As disclosed by the high-angle annular dark-field scanning (HAADF) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping images (Fig. 2d), further demonstrating the homogeneous distribution of C, N, O and S elements within the as-prepared LCDs substrate. As depicted in Fig. 2e, FTIR spectra interpret the information about functional groups on the surface of LCDs that refer to their solubility differences. Although these samples exhibited similar absorption peaks of ∼ 3300–3500 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, and 800 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of –OH/−NH, C=O, C=N, C–S/C=S groups, respectively [39]. In contrast, the intensity variations at ∼ 1100 cm−1 are attributed to hydrophilic C−NH groups, affirming the discrepancy in water solubility. The species of DCDs exhibit the intense bands at ∼ 2900 cm−1, representing the hydrophobic alkyl chains and/or phenyl rings. Additionally, the differentia C=C bond intensities (the aromatic skeleton at 1680 cm−1) of L50-DCDs, L100-DCDs and LR-DCDs illustrate that the lignin structure affects the extractability in CH2Cl2. Moreover, these distinguished signals arisen from conjugated and aromatic systems in DCDs provide the evidences of their hydrophobicity and PL emission in near-infrared region [40]. Both LR-DCDs and L50-FCDs present the typical carbon characteristics with well-defined defect (D-band) and graphite (G-band) domains in the Raman spectra (Fig. 2f). The relative proportion of G to D intensities (I G/I D) for LR-DCDs is calculated to be 1.19, which is slightly higher than that of L50-FCDs (1.15), further confirming the graphitization degree of DCDs to a large extent. The zeta potentials of the selected L100-WCDs and L50-FCDs are determined in Fig. 2g, in which the L50-FCDs have a higher ζ potential value (−25.37 mV) than that of L100-WCDs (−15.03 mV). The strong electrostatic repulsion of LCDs endows them with excellent stability in aqueous solutions. This result might consistent with the content of –COOH that holds the potential of deprotonation.

As measured by atomic force microscope (AFM) in Fig. 3 a-c and the 3D height patterns in Fig. S2, the uniform height distributions of L50-FCDs, L100-FCDs, and L50-DCDs are confined within the range from 3 to 10 nm, which is in good agreement with the TEM results. The accurate elemental constituents of as-prepared LCDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Full survey XPS spectra of the selected LR-FCDs (Fig. 3d) and L50-FCDs (Fig. S3a) present the specific signals of four elements including C, N, O and S, whereas the L100-DCDs (Fig. S3b) and LR-DCDs contain C, O and S (Fig. S3c). As a rule, the lignin fractions with the smaller M w have more reactive hydroxyl and/or carboxyl groups that are peculiarly prone to binding with amino and sulfonate, thereby resulting in the impactful co-doping of N, S into aromatic molecules. On the other side, this result also potentially demonstrates that the N-related dopants and groups are difficult to embed within the lignin macromolecules through weak sulfanilic acidolysis. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e) of LR-FCDs can be fitted into four peaks, referring to C−O/C−N (49.11 %), C=N (14.11 %), C=C (28.39 %) and C=O (8.39 %). The oxygen species in the LR-FCDs (Fig. 3f) and L50-FCDs (Fig. S3e) are mainly classified into two environments including C=O (∼531.8 eV) and C−O (∼533.0 eV). The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of LR-FCDs (Fig. 3g) and L50-FCDs (Fig. S3f) are deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to amino N (∼400 eV) and graphitic N (∼402.2 eV). It is noteworthy that the high-resolution S 2p XPS profile of L50-FCDs (Fig. S3g) exhibits an addition peak of C-SO2 as compared with LR-FCDs (Fig. 3h), except for C-SO3 and C-SO4 bands. The presence of sulfone groups in LCDs can be ascribed to the in-situ and/or acidolysis doping of alkali lignin. The optical absorption of the LCDs was analyzed via UV–vis diffusion reflectance spectra (DRS) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. As displayed in Fig. S4, the absorption intensity of L100-DCDs in the visible light region is strengthened relative to that of L50-FCDs. The band-gap energies (E g) can be calculated using the Tauc plots of (α×hν)1/n = A(hν–Eg); wherein α, hν, and A represents the absorption coefficient, energy, and constant value respectively, and the quantity of n is taken as 2 [41,42]. From Fig. 3i, the band-gap energy of L100-DCDs (3.27 eV) is slight lower than that of L50-FCDs (3.30 eV), corresponding to the red-shifted emission wavelength of DCDs as compared with FCDs.

We investigate the optical performances of as-prepared LCDs using the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. Fig. S5 shows that the absorption peaks in the range of 250–400 nm are mainly triggered by the coordination between the π-π* transition of the sp 2 aromatics and the n-π* transition of the surface-dependent active species (e.g., C–N, C=O and S=O) [43,44]. The optimum excitation and emission peaks of all the LCDs are located within the range of 360–390 nm/450–500 nm (FCDs), 320–330 nm/420–450 nm (WCDs), and 540–600 nm/640–690 nm (DCDs), accompanied by bright blue, green, and red emitting under UV-irradiation respectively. Furthermore, the fractionated lignin structures possess the potential influences on the photophysical properties (e.g., emission wavelength, stokes shift, and full width at half maximum) of their derivative LCDs. It is noteworthy that the DCDs (Fig. 4 a-c), FCDs (Fig. S6) and WCDs (Fig. S7) display excitation-dependent characteristics, potentially referring to the presence of narrow size distribution and homogeneous emission condition in the LCDs. It's wonder that a visible bimodal emission can be observed from the LR-FCDs (Fig. S6c), suggesting the presence of several different optical centers [45,46]. More importantly, the emission wavelengths of FCDs are gradually reduced with the increase of lignin M w values from L50 to LR. A plausible explanation is that the increased M w of lignin fractions is always accompanied by the weakened behavior of depolymerization and decreased content of –OH/COOH groups, thus resulting in the nucleation of FCDs with low oxygen-containing functional groups and/or structural defects capable of blue-shift emission. However, the similar red-emission wavelengths of the CH2Cl2-phase DCDs are observed. After acidolysis, the lignin structure tends to homogenization (e.g., average molecular weights and surface groups) via acid stripping and surface passivation. Their homothetic interface modification and nanoparticle sizes are likely to enter the water or CH2Cl2-phase separately, leading to the insignificant change of luminescence properties with varied lignin fractions. The 3D PL excitation-emission mappings of FCDs (Fig. S6d-f), WCDs (Fig. S7d-f) and DCDs (Fig. 4d-f) are plotted to further demonstrate the independence relationship for as-prepared LCDs dispersed in ethanol, water and CH2Cl2, respectively. In different solvents, Fig. 4g-i presents the PL decays of LCDs with varying lignin structures and the average PL lifetimes are calculated from bi-exponential fits. Time-resolved photoluminescence is a suitable technique to potentially address the mechanisms of energy and/or charge transfer among functional groups or molecular interactions. It is witnessed that the PL lifetimes of LCDs is prolonged with the increase of lignin M w values. Accompanied by the high M w with lengthened molecular chain and oxygen-related species (especially of β aryl ethers), is susceptible to affect the reactivity and size in the nucleation of CDs [24,25]. Generally, the plots of fluorescence intensity decay reveal the kinetics of the deactivation of electrons from an excited state to the ground state via radiative and non-radiative pathways [43,47]. It is likely to support the occurrence of complex structures in LCDs-interacted large lignin molecules, in which inner states strongly interact with surface states. The electrons need consume more time in the excited state as a consequence of the close connection with extended lignin aromatic chains (corresponding to high Mw values), thereby leading to a longer fluorescence lifetime. In acidolysis filtrate, their average PL lifetime is gradually lengthened from 2.92 to 13.12 ns. After acidolysis, the PL lifetimes decrease to 0.5–2.19 ns in water and 2.52–3.51 ns in CH2Cl2. We notice that these decay changes in the polar solvent are expected to ignite a shift of the excited state energy via interacting solvent dipoles with a fluorophore and the extended relaxation, which is typically manifested as a PL band red-shift occurring with an increase of solvent polarity [21].

The escalating exposure of toxic metal-ions and pollutants to the human body via directly drinking water has threatened the public and individual health [48]. In this vein, discovering LCDs in the practical environmental remediation has gained awareness because of their tunable selectivity, sensitivity, limit of detection (LOD), and linear concentration range [49]. During the past decade, deriving CDs from lignocellulose has been applied to detecting metal-ions in water [46,50,51], such as lotus root for Hg2+, stem of banana plant for Fe3+, bamboo leaves for Cu2+, coriander leaves for Fe3+, peanut shells for Cu2+, papaya waste for Cr3+/Cr6+, biorefinery residue consisting of sugars and depolymerized lignin for Fe3+, hemicellulose for Ag+ (Table S1). As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the PL sensing performances of L50-FCDs, L100-FCDs and LR-FCDs with the presence of tetracycline (TC) are plotted ranging from 0–500 μM, wherein the photoluminescence quenching response can be built to detect TC concentration in water. Accompanied by the general increase of TC content, we clearly observe that the fluorescent intensities of fixed emission wavelength are weakened for L50-FCDs (Fig. 5a), L100-FCDs (Fig. 5b) and LR-FCDs (Fig. 5c), as a consequence of the plausible internal filtration effect (IFE). The linear correlations are fitted over TC concentration of 0–50 μm capable of diverse coefficients and LOD with respect to L50-FCDs (Fig. 5d, R 2 = 0.994, LOD = 1.27 μM), L100-FCDs (Fig. 5e, R 2 = 0.984, LOD = 1.32 μM) and LR-FCDs (Fig. 5f, R 2 = 0.9979, LOD = 0.66 μM). The different metal-ions (Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Ag+ and Ni2+) with a concentration of 500 μM are also selected to launch the fluorescent identification at a fixed excitation wavelength. We observe that the fluorescence intensity of L50-WCDs can be rapidly quenched and strengthened via the action of Fe3+ and Al3+ ions, respectively (Fig. 6 a-f), whereas the fluorescences of L100-WCDs (Fig. 6g-i) and LR-WCDs (Fig. 6j-l) are quenched significantly with the adding Fe3+ and Ag+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of as-synthesized LCDs with different concentrations of metal-ions is also studied to evaluate the LOD according to the formula LOD = 3σ/S (σ represents the standard deviation and S defines the slope of the fitting line). As depicted in Fig. 6c, we establish a fluorescent Fe3+-ions sensing ability of the L50-WCDs in a concentration from 0 to 100 μM with a high linear coefficient of R2 0.998, and the LOD is calculated to be 3.49 μM. In contrast, a luminescent reinforcing linear function is found with the coefficient of R2 = 0.991 and the LOD of 0.60 μM (Fig. 6f). In a concentration of 0–60 μM, the L100-WCDs allow a sensing ability of Fe3+ ions with a linear coefficient of R2 0.98314 and an identical LOD (Fig. 6i). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of LR-WCDs gradually quenched with the increasing of Ag+-ions from 0 to 600 μM (Fig. 6k), which exhibits a selectively linear sensing capacity with a ultralow LOD of 285.22 nM upon a wide Ag+ concentration of 0–200 μM (coefficient R2 0.9871) (Fig. 6l). The surface functional groups of WCDs hold the distinctive cheating abilities of metal-ions with tunable ionic radii and surface charges, wherein shaping the heteroatom-doped groups (e.g., O, N and S) into a stable complex and/or coordination, thereby leading to the fluorescent quenching or reinforcing of the selected WCDs with diversified selectivity and sensitivity. In specific, the fluorescence quenching behaviors of WCDs are mainly attributed to the arrangement of surface oxygen-containing groups on their surface with ionized Fe3+ and/or Ag+. The addition of Al3+ might cause the partially surface aggregation of L50-WCDs, thereby reinforcing their fluorescence through a so-called aggregation-induced enhancement of emission (AIEE) [49].

To further disclose the possibility of LCDs in constructing solid-luminescent films, we propose a solvent-casting approach via adding red-luminescent L50-DCDs with 0.5 wt% into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix for the fabrication of active food packaging applications. Owing to a higher content of –OH and –COOH in the L50 as compared with L100 and LR (Fig. 1d), hence the robust hydrogen-bond binding abilities could be imported into the L50-DCDs that create an idea compatibility with PVA. As revealed by the surface chemistry and heteroatom composition in the XPS spectrum of the hybrid films (Fig. 7 a), the C, O and S are presented on the surface of PVA-LCDs composites, while the N peak is extremely unobvious due to its weak doping in L50-DCDs. In Fig. 7b of the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum, the nonoxygenated (C=C/C–C), carbonyl (C=O) and heteroatom-doped (C=N/C=S) carbons can be classified with a semi-quantitative approach. And their high-resolution O 1 s (Fig. 7c) and S 2p spectra is almost analogical to the L50-FCDs, which could be divided into C–O/C=O and C–SO x compositions respectively. Promisingly, the O, S, N-doping is of importance to ameliorate the wettability of as-fabricated PVA-LCDs, thereby not only alleviating their interface contact resistance but also endowing the moisturizing ability to store food. As displayed in Fig. 7d, the feasibility to altering the ordered patterns of as-fabricated PVA-LCDs films are achieved via a facile evaporation-induced self-assembly, allowing a solid-state luminescence behavior over ultraviolet on/off. We conceived a cross-linked quasi-supramolecular network by hydrogen-bonding (Fig. 7e), providing a compact structure to incorporate L50-DCDs within PVA matrix phase. A good hydrophilicity of PVA-LCDs films is affirmed by the small water contact angle of ∼ 28.5° (Fig. 7f), which guarantees the wettability of food storage. As shown in SEM images (Fig. 7g), the neat PVA-LCDs films have a continuous, smooth and dense surface with the absence of obvious impurities, voids and/or cracks, further disclosing the excellent alignment between nanoscale DCDs with PVA polymer. This might be ascribed to the strong interaction of DCDs with PVA via intermolecular hydrogen bonding and the homogeneous dispersion as a function of their hydrophilic structure. The crystallinity of functional films directly deal with the amorphous nature of polymeric PVA and graphite L50-DCDs, owing to a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20° as a result of the increased crosslinking intensity (Fig. 7h). To verify the practical applicability of the PVA-LCDs packaging films, we conducted a fruit preservation experiment on the perishable strawberry (Fig. 7i). Using the pure PVA films, we observed that the moisture content of strawberries was decreased and their deteriorated surface state became mildewed distinctly, accompanied by lengthening the storage time to ninth day. In contrast, the strawberry fruits maintained fresher and bright-red in the PVA-LCDs films with the absence of notable oxidation or dehydration on the day 9. The above results illustrate that the developed PVA-LCDs composites can persist the appearance of strawberries with the limited decay for a long time, thereby effectively extending the freshness of the wrapped perishable fruit. As the absorbance of the film increased, the PVA-LCDs film effectively blocks the UV rays to entering the fruit (Fig. S8a) that demonstrates the potential of retarding lipid oxidation in photosensitive foods. The enhanced absorbance of PVA-LCDs composites also can ameliorate their thermal resistance and photodynamic antibacterial ability, thereby prolonging the shelf-life of strawberry [52]. In Fig. S8b, the pure PVA film shows a negligible scavenging effect on DPPH free radicals (∼4%). The improved scavenging rate of PVA-LCDs (∼36 %) illustrates that incorporation of DCDs endows the composite films with strong antioxidant activity, which is due to the contribution of electron donor groups (e.g., hydroxyl and carboxyl) from DCDs to the interface. A lower oxygen permeability (OP) of hybrid film also affirms its excellent oxygen barrier performance owing to the strong hydrogen bonding and multiple non-covalent stacking between PVA and DCDs (Fig. S8c), which could introduce a physical obstruction to diffusing oxygen molecules. We thus conclude that constructing well-ordered lignin structures and their related multicolor LCDs from blue to red can be fulfilled by a dynamic solvent-fractionation engineering with gradient ethanol and CH2Cl2/water extraction, which strides the threshold of multifunctional luminescent materials ranging from complex wastewater monitoring to food packaging films via the sustainable and low-cost lignin building blocks.

Deriving multicolor shining LCDs from the refined lignin with a desired, tailor-made structure allows the multifaceted functionalities in a sustainable path, yet realizing the tunable wide-band and red-luminescence confronts a challenging. By dynamically changing the gradient ethanol in lignin fractionation, the M w values, −OH/COOH contents, and homogeneous structure of lignin fractions are systematically controlled, thereby correlating the reactive paths and physicochemical properties of the derivative LCDs. Coupling the CH2Cl2/water extraction, the selected lignin and adjustable solvents are both of importance to produce three emission LCDs (blue, yellow and red), which disclose that the disparity in luminescent color and lifetime of LCDs is mainly due to the size, microenvironment, band-gap energies, and functional groups of the sp 2 conjugate domain. Among them, FCDs and WCDs can recognize TC (coefficient R 2 0.984–9979 and LOD of 0.66–1.32 μM) and metal-ions (Fe3+, Al3+ and/or Ag + with the differential LOD values of up to 3.49 μM) with high selectivity and linear accuracy in the varied concentrations, respectively. Solid-state fluorescence films are facilely fabricated via mixing red-emissive DCDs with PVA matrices, revealing a potential anti-oxidation function of preserving fruits. The present work not only opens a new direction to scalable engineering multicolor LCDs with blue, green and red emitting, but also delivers a ternary systems of fulfilling bimodule fluorescent detection, solid-state luminescent films, and multiscale biorefining industries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 22008159), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project (2022020801020312), and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018T110770).

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
